questions.question_text: Which symbol makes the statement true? $-6 \; \square \; -3$

options.why: judged $-6$ as greater because its digit $6$ is larger than $3$
treated both negatives as the same value since they share a $-$ sign

questions.solution_text:
$-6$ is to the left of $-3$ on the number line, so it is less.
$-6 < -3$

questions.question_text: Which of these integers is the greatest? $-8, \; -3, \; -5$

options.why: chose the integer with the largest digit, ignoring that it is furthest left
picked a middle value, comparing by digit size rather than position

questions.solution_text:
The greatest integer is furthest right on the number line.
$-3$ is closest to zero, so it is the greatest.

questions.question_text: Which list shows $-13, \; 6, \; -2, \; 11, \; -7$ in ascending order (smallest to largest)?

options.why: ordered the negatives by digit size, smallest digit first, instead of by position
arranged the list in descending order instead of ascending
placed the positives first, treating negatives as larger

questions.solution_text:
Negatives from furthest left: $-13$, $-7$, $-2$; then positives $6$, $11$.
$-13, \; -7, \; -2, \; 6, \; 11$

questions.question_text: Which list shows $-4, \; 2, \; -9, \; 0, \; 5$ in descending order (largest to smallest)?

options.why: listed the integers in ascending order instead of descending
ordered the negatives by digit size, placing $-9$ before $-4$
treated the larger-digit negatives as the largest values

questions.solution_text:
Largest first: $5$, $2$, $0$; then negatives closest to zero first: $-4$, $-9$.
$5, \; 2, \; 0, \; -4, \; -9$

questions.question_text: Which list gives all integers that are greater than $-65$ and less than $-58$?

options.why: included both endpoints, treating 'greater than' and 'less than' as inclusive
included $-58$, though it is not less than $-58$
excluded $-64$, starting two above $-65$ instead of one

questions.question_text: The temperature in Warsaw is warmer than Moscow's $-8^{\circ}\text{C}$ but colder than Toronto's $-3^{\circ}\text{C}$. Which could be Warsaw's temperature?

options.why: $-9$ is colder than $-8$, so it is not warmer than Moscow
$-2$ is warmer than $-3$, so it is not colder than Toronto
$-8$ equals Moscow's temperature, not strictly warmer than it

questions.solution_text:
Warsaw lies strictly between $-8$ and $-3$: $-8 < \text{Warsaw} < -3$.
$-5^{\circ}\text{C}$ works, as does $-7$, $-6$ or $-4$.

questions.question_text: Which symbol makes the statement true? $0 \; \square \; -100$

options.why: assumed $-100$ is greater because $100$ is a large number
unsure how zero compares to a negative, treating them as equal

questions.solution_text:
Every negative integer is less than $0$, so $0$ is to the right of $-100$.
$0 > -100$
